turns.content: I'm trying to understand how to split a stream of sensor readings into two separate vectors, one for values above a threshold and another for the rest. I've heard you can use an iterator method for this instead of collecting everything first.

You are looking at the partition method on iterators which takes a predicate closure and yields two iterators: one containing elements where your predicate returns true, and another for false. This avoids materializing the full collection in memory before splitting it. If you want to group adjacent identical readings instead of filtering by value, you would use the group_by method which groups consecutive equal items into tuples of (key, iterator).

That makes sense for partitioning, but I'm running into an edge case where my closure returns true only on even indices, and I need to know exactly how many elements went into each bucket without storing all of them. Can you explain the difference between using partition with a simple predicate versus using group_by when my data has runs of identical values?

Partition simply forwards every element that matches your closure to one output stream and ignores it for the other, so if your predicate depends on index you cannot use it directly unless you wrap the iterator in something like a Peekable or collect into a Vec first. Group_by is specifically designed to group adjacent equal elements into a vector of (key, iterator) tuples where each key repeats until the next distinct value appears; this is useful when you want to compress consecutive duplicates rather than filter by condition.
I'm struggling with Rust iterators, specifically the difference between partition and group_by. I know I've used vec and iter before, but how do these actually fit into that?

The key distinction is that partition splits a stream into two separate collections based on a predicate, while group_by clusters adjacent identical elements. When you use partition with an impl IntoIterator trait, it creates vecs where one holds items matching the condition and the other holds those that don't. In contrast, group_by groups consecutive duplicates together, which is useful for counting runs of values without needing to sort first.
So I can just call these methods directly on an iterator? Like if I have a vec of strings, I could split them by length?

Exactly. If you define a predicate that checks the byte length of each string and apply partition, it will yield two vecs: one with short names and another with long ones. However, group_by won't do this because it only groups adjacent equal values; if your input isn't sorted by length, group_by will just create a single group containing all strings since they are never identical.
Ah, I see. But what if I want to count how many times each character appears in a sentence?

That's an excellent use case for group_by. You can iterate through the characters, and since you're grouping adjacent identical elements, every run of 'a's will be collected into one vec. Then you map over those groups to count their length, effectively tallying each unique character sequence.
That makes sense now. Can I still use partition for counting instead?

Not really, because partition doesn't group adjacent duplicates together; it only separates items based on a boolean condition. For your character count scenario, you'd need to ensure the stream is sorted by character value first if using partition logic, which defeats the purpose of grouping nearby runs.

Hey! I'm new to Rust and looking at a pull request that uses iterators. It seems like the code is splitting my data into two separate lists based on some condition, but I don't understand what partition does versus group_by.

Hey there! That's a great question for someone just starting out with Rust. Think of partition as taking an iterator and filtering it into two distinct collections at once, like separating apples from oranges using one pass through the line. In contrast, group_by is more about organizing adjacent items that are equal together, which is useful when you want to count runs or find consecutive duplicates instead.
Oh, okay so partition is like a filter but splitting into two buckets? And if I wanted to group similar values next to each other, would that be partition too?

You got it! Partition uses a predicate function to decide whether each element goes to the first vec or the second vec, so you end up with two separate collections. If your goal is grouping adjacent equal elements together in one collection, then group_by is what you want because it collects consecutive runs of identical values.
So partition gives me two vecs while group_by keeps them all in one? Can I use these on any kind of iterator or only specific ones?

Exactly, both partition and group_by work with generic iterators as long as you can provide a predicate for partition or a key function for grouping. They are designed to be flexible so they fit many different scenarios in your code.
Wait, so if I have a list of numbers like 1,2,2,3,3,4 and I use group_by on it, what would the result look like?
If you apply group_by to that sequence, you get groups of consecutive duplicates: [1], [2, 2], [3, 3], and [4]. Each group contains only items that appeared consecutively in the original list.

I've read about iterators multiple times but still don't fully understand how to split a stream into two separate collections using a simple condition. I keep thinking the Vec::split method does this, which is completely wrong since that method only works on strings or vectors of tuples and returns nothing useful for my use case.

You are correct that there is no split method on Vec or iterators that does what you need; instead you should use the partition method from std::iter. This trait takes a closure and yields one iterator where each element either satisfies your predicate and goes into an accumulator, or fails it and goes into another accumulator. For example if you want to separate odd and even numbers you would write something like let (evens, odds) = numbers.partition(|n| n % 2 == 0); which produces two iterators that you can then collect into Vec<i32> using the from_iter method or convert them manually with for loops.

That sounds perfect but I still don't understand how this relates to grouping adjacent equal elements like in my game inventory system where I want to compress a list of items so duplicate rows only appear once. I thought group_by would just sort and count, which isn't what I need because I am not sorting the data first.

You are right that group_by sorts by key before grouping but for your specific case you do not need sorting since you want to preserve original order while collapsing consecutive duplicates. Instead of using group_by you should use a simple loop with an iterator chain where you keep track of the previous item and only emit it if it differs from the last one or collect it into a separate buffer. You can implement this by iterating over the input and comparing each element to the previous one in the same collection, then appending distinct items to your result vector.

I think I finally see how these two concepts differ since partition splits based on a boolean predicate while group_by relies on equality keys after sorting. But wait doesn't partition also use some kind of key function internally? Isn't that why it feels similar to grouping by value even though it returns different types?

Partition does not use any key function at all; it simply evaluates your closure against each element and directs the result to one of two output streams based on true or false. It never calculates a hash or compares values like group_by does which requires sorting first by the key field. Your partition logic is purely conditional without any reordering while group_by fundamentally depends on ordering elements so that identical keys end up adjacent before being grouped together.

Ah I see now! Partition gives me two parallel streams immediately without touching the original order whereas group_by scrambles everything based on the key. So for my inventory system where I just want to remove consecutive duplicates in place partition won't help because it needs a different pattern than what I was thinking but that's actually exactly why I should use the manual loop approach with an if statement checking against the previous item instead.

Exactly! You are now clear on how these tools differ and when to apply each one. Partition splits data into two buckets based on a condition without reordering, while group_by requires sorting first then condenses equal keys. For your inventory deduplication you need neither of those since you only care about adjacent duplicates in their original sequence. Your manual loop approach using an iterator chain with a conditional check against the previous value is the correct solution and will compress your list efficiently without any sorting overhead.
